narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 38—above the typical 36.7 average—and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is slightly below the usual 3.21. However, the university's admission model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 76% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight in the model's decision. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower end of your peers, a pattern strongly linked to bar struggles in similar students. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 3, again under the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about consistently weaker early rankings compared to those who pass the bar. On a more positive note, your above-average LSAT score worked against the failure prediction, pulling the odds in your favor more than most applicants' scores do. Overall, your situation compares to typical admitted students by having brighter spots like the LSAT and full-time commitment, but your lower decile rankings in both early and later years deviated notably from the stronger academic trajectories that predict bar passage. While disheartening, this isn't a final verdict on your potential—many factors beyond these metrics shape real-world success. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA improvement initiative, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bolstering your narrative around that LSAT strength. With focused effort on those early academic flags, your profile could shift dramatically for future cycles. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for determined applicants like you.
